questions.question: Describe the process of inserting a new node with the value '25' into the following Binary Search Tree (BST). Your answer should detail the steps of traversal and the rules followed to maintain the BST property. Root: 50 (Left: 30, Right: 70); Node 30 (Left: 20, Right: 40); Node 70 (Left: 60, Right: 80).
keyPoints: Start at the root (50). Compare 25 < 50, so move to the left child (30).
Compare 25 < 30, so move to the left child of 30 (20).
Compare 25 > 20. Since the right child of 20 is null, insert 25 as the new right child of node 20.
The BST property is maintained as all left descendants of a node are less, and all right descendants are greater.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the time complexity of the search operation in a Binary Search Tree (BST) under its best-case and worst-case scenarios. Explain what structural properties of the tree lead to each scenario.
keyPoints: Best-case (balanced tree): Time complexity is O(log n). This occurs when the tree is approximately balanced, meaning the height is logarithmic to the number of nodes (n).
Worst-case (unbalanced tree, e.g., a degenerate tree/linked list): Time complexity is O(n). This occurs when nodes are inserted in sorted order, causing the tree to have a height equal to n.
The key structural property determining efficiency is the height of the tree. A lower height leads to efficient O(log n) operations, while a height of n leads to inefficient O(n) operations.

questions.question: Trace and explain the complete process of deleting a node with two children from a Binary Search Tree. Use the example of deleting the root node '50' from the following BST: Root: 50 (Left: 30, Right: 70); Node 30 (Left: 20, Right: 40); Node 70 (Left: 60, Right: 80). Your answer must detail the two-step process of finding the successor and restructuring the tree.
keyPoints: To delete a node with two children (50), find its in-order successor. This is the smallest node in its right subtree.
Traverse right once (to 70), then repeatedly traverse left until a node with no left child is found. The in-order successor of 50 is 60.
Copy the value of the successor (60) into the node to be deleted. The node 50 now has the value 60.
Delete the original successor node (now redundant value 60). Since this node had a right child (80), the right child takes the place of the deleted successor node.
